refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires clarification of scope (what constitutes 'worksite model'), measurement basis (p95 latency metric), and boundary conditions (supported scenarios). High safety criticality demands explicit traceability to safety-critical update types (e.g., hazard alerts, position updates for collision avoidance). Refined FR anchors acceptance to measurable latency SLA and acknowledges customer tailoring.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument telemetry pipeline and worksite model update subsystem with end-to-end latency probes. Collect p95 latency across representative scenarios (equipment position updates, sensor alerts, tele-operator commands, safety events). Compare against customer-defined target and acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire real-time telemetry from vehicle sensors and tele-operator inputs with minimal capture latency.
FR-007.2: Transmit telemetry and state updates over network link with bounded network latency.
FR-007.3: Process and fuse incoming telemetry into worksite model representation with minimal computation latency.
FR-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) with minimal delivery latency.
FR-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (hazard alerts, collision warnings) to meet stricter latency bounds than non-critical updates.

candidate_dps: DP-007.A: Implement end-to-end latency budget allocation across telemetry acquisition, network transmission, model processing, and distribution; use priority queuing for safety-critical updates.
DP-007.B: Deploy edge processing on vehicle to pre-filter and compress telemetry before transmission; centralize model fusion in cloud backend.
DP-007.C: Implement multi-tier caching and predictive model updates to reduce perceived latency; use delta-encoding for incremental updates.
DP-007.D: Establish dedicated low-latency network path for safety-critical updates; use standard network for non-critical telemetry.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-007.A: Implement end-to-end latency budget allocation across telemetry acquisition, network transmission, model processing, and distribution; use priority queuing for safety-critical updates. This DP directly addresses the p95 latency constraint by decomposing the problem into measurable sub-paths and introducing safety-aware prioritization aligned with high safety criticality.

potential_sources: Latency budget allocation depends on network infrastructure characteristics (DP-Network Link design).
Priority queuing logic couples with Safety Services module for hazard classification.
Model processing latency couples with data schema and fusion algorithm complexity (DP-Model Design).
coupling_check.notes: DP-007.A is loosely coupled to physical vehicle design and tightly coupled to software architecture (telemetry pipeline, queuing, model update logic). Coupling is manageable via interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and latency SLAs. Recommend explicit latency budget document as design artifact.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network link latency is predictable and bounded; risk: variable network conditions may violate latency budget.
Assumption: Safety-critical update classification is deterministic and available at ingestion; risk: misclassification may delay critical alerts.
Assumption: Model processing (fusion, state update) can be optimized to meet allocated latency budget; risk: algorithmic complexity may exceed budget.
Assumption: Consumers can tolerate eventual consistency for non-critical updates; risk: stale model state may cause operational confusion.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-007.A to explicitly specify: (1) latency budget breakdown by sub-path (acquisition, network, processing, delivery), (2) safety-critical update classification criteria, (3) fallback strategy if latency budget is exceeded, (4) monitoring and alerting for latency SLA violations.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: End-to-end worksite model update pipeline, including telemetry ingestion, network transmission coordination, model fusion and state management, and distribution to authorized consumers. Encompasses latency budget allocation, priority queuing, and safety-critical update handling across all sub-systems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle (sensor data, state updates) and Teleoperation (operator commands, tele-operator state). Carries raw and processed sensor streams with timestamps.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound task assignments and operational directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems based on worksite model state and Site Manager decisions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety event communication: inbound hazard alerts from Safety Services; outbound safety-critical updates (collision warnings, equipment state changes) to Safety Services for validation and escalation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Network transport for telemetry, tasking, and safety alerts between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution backend. Latency characteristics of this link directly impact end-to-end update latency.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration point for all worksite model update logic. Latency budget allocation, priority queuing, and model fusion are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only network transport (Network Link interface) and safety classification (Safety Services interface) are external dependencies.

Vehicle is responsible for telemetry acquisition and transmission, but not for model fusion or distribution. Latency budget for vehicle-side acquisition is a sub-component of overall latency. High diffusion: vehicle design is independent of worksite model update logic; coupling is via Telemetry interface only.

Teleoperation provides operator input and receives updated worksite model for display. Not responsible for model fusion or latency budget allocation. High diffusion: tele-operator interface is decoupled from backend model update logic.

Safety Services is responsible for hazard classification and safety-critical update prioritization. Contributes to latency budget via safety alert processing. Moderate diffusion: safety logic is specialized and may be reused across multiple update scenarios; coupling via SafetyAlert interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner for end-to-end latency budget allocation and worksite model update orchestration. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are specialized sub-systems that contribute to latency via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Latency budget allocation and priority queuing logic should be centralized in NGHE Solution to avoid diffusion and ensure coherent SLA management.
containment_risks: Risk: If latency budget allocation logic is distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, priority queuing may become incoherent and SLA violations may be difficult to diagnose.
Risk: If model fusion algorithm is not optimized, processing latency may exceed allocated budget, causing cascading delays.
Risk: If network link characteristics are not well-characterized, latency budget allocation may be unrealistic.
Risk: If safety-critical update classification is ambiguous, misclassification may delay critical alerts or over-prioritize non-critical updates.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit latency budget document owned by NGHE Solution team. Define: (1) end-to-end latency SLA (p95 ≤ 1.0 s), (2) sub-path latency budgets (acquisition, network, processing, delivery), (3) safety-critical update classification criteria, (4) priority queue configuration, (5) monitoring and alerting thresholds. Assign ownership of each sub-path to responsible module (Vehicle for acquisition, Network Link for transmission, NGHE Solution for processing/delivery). Establish interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with latency guarantees.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-007.A (latency budget allocation with priority queuing) directly addresses FR-007 (worksite model update latency ≤ 1.0 s p95). DP is measurable, testable, and safety-aware. Coupling is manageable via interface contracts. Recommended revision to DP (explicit budget breakdown, fallback strategy) should be incorporated before detailed design.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If latency budget allocation logic is not centralized in NGHE Solution, priority queuing may become incoherent across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
Diffusion risk: If model fusion algorithm is distributed across multiple modules, optimization and debugging become difficult.
Diffusion risk: If network latency characteristics are not well-characterized, latency budget allocation may be unrealistic and require rework.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner for end-to-end latency orchestration. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services contribute via well-defined interfaces. Containment score of 95 for NGHE Solution is justified. Recommend establishing explicit latency budget document and interface contracts to minimize diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumentation of telemetry pipeline and worksite model update subsystem. End-to-end latency can be measured across representative scenarios. p95 latency can be compared against customer-defined target. Recommend establishing latency monitoring and alerting in production.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns end-to-end latency budget allocation and priority queuing. Vehicle owns telemetry acquisition latency. Network Link owns transmission latency. Safety Services owns hazard classification latency. Recommend explicit latency budget document to formalize allocation.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-007.A to include explicit latency budget breakdown by sub-path (acquisition, network, processing, delivery).
Establish latency budget document owned by NGHE Solution team, with sub-path budgets assigned to responsible modules.
Define safety-critical update classification criteria and priority queue configuration.
Establish interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with latency guarantees.
Plan latency monitoring and alerting for production deployment.
Conduct network characterization study to validate latency budget assumptions.

decision.justification: FR-007 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs (FR-007.1 through FR-007.5) to address telemetry acquisition, network transmission, model processing, distribution, and safety prioritization. DP-007.A is sound and well-aligned with NGHE Solution module, but requires refinement (explicit budget breakdown, fallback strategy, monitoring plan) before detailed design. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level for each sub-FR to allocate latency budgets and design sub-system interfaces.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR identifies five distinct sub-problems: (1) telemetry acquisition latency, (2) network transmission latency, (3) model processing latency, (4) distribution latency, (5) safety-critical prioritization. Each sub-FR requires its own DP and IM assignment. DP-007.A provides the overarching strategy (latency budget allocation with priority queuing), but does not specify how each sub-path will achieve its budget. Recommend decomposing to next level to design telemetry pipeline, network protocol, model fusion algorithm, and distribution mechanism.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-007.1: Acquire real-time telemetry from vehicle sensors and tele-operator inputs with minimal capture latency (target: ≤ 50 ms p95).
FR-007.2: Transmit telemetry and state updates over network link with bounded network latency (target: ≤ 300 ms p95, customer-dependent).
FR-007.3: Process and fuse incoming telemetry into worksite model representation with minimal computation latency (target: ≤ 200 ms p95).
FR-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) with minimal delivery latency (target: ≤ 200 ms p95).
FR-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (hazard alerts, collision warnings) to meet stricter latency bounds than non-critical updates (target: ≤ 500 ms p95 for safety-critical).

assumptions: Network link latency is predictable and bounded within customer-defined SLA.
Safety-critical update classification is deterministic and available at telemetry ingestion.
Model processing (fusion, state update) can be optimized to meet allocated latency budget.
Consumers can tolerate eventual consistency for non-critical updates.
Telemetry acquisition on vehicle can be optimized to meet sub-budget (≤ 50 ms p95).
Network transmission can be optimized via compression, prioritization, and protocol selection.
Model fusion algorithm can be optimized to meet sub-budget (≤ 200 ms p95).
Distribution mechanism can be optimized to meet sub-budget (≤ 200 ms p95).
open_risks: Network latency variability may exceed allocated budget under adverse conditions (congestion, packet loss).
Safety-critical update misclassification may delay critical alerts or over-prioritize non-critical updates.
Model fusion algorithm complexity may exceed allocated latency budget, requiring algorithmic optimization or hardware acceleration.
Telemetry volume may exceed network capacity, requiring compression or filtering strategies.
Worksite model state consistency may be difficult to maintain under high update rates, requiring conflict resolution logic.
Monitoring and alerting infrastructure may not be in place to detect latency SLA violations in production.
evidence_needed: Network characterization study: measure latency distribution (mean, p50, p95, p99) under representative load conditions.
Telemetry volume analysis: quantify sensor data rates and update frequencies from Vehicle and Teleoperation.
Model fusion algorithm performance analysis: measure processing latency for representative worksite model sizes and update rates.
Safety-critical update classification criteria: define hazard types and prioritization rules.
Latency budget validation: conduct end-to-end latency measurement across representative scenarios and compare against customer-defined target.
Monitoring and alerting design: specify latency metrics, thresholds, and alerting mechanisms for production deployment.
